7153.text: (2) Where any marking or writing in colour on a document is important, the document must be copied in colour or marked up correctly in colour.

7154.text: (3) Documents which are not easily legible should be transcribed and the transcription marked and placed adjacent to the document transcribed.

7155.text: (4) Documents in a language other than English should be translated and the translation marked and placed adjacent to the document translated. The translation should be agreed or, if it cannot be agreed, each party’s proposed translation should be included.

7156.text: (5) The size of any bundle should be tailored to its contents. A large lever arch file should not be used for just a few pages nor should files be overloaded.

7157.text: (6) Where it will assist the CourtG , different sections of the file may be separated by cardboard or other tabbed dividers so long as these are clearly indexed. Where, for example, a document is awaited when the appeal bundle is filed, a single sheet of paper can be inserted after a divider, indicating the nature of the document awaited. For example, ‘Transcript of evidence of Mr J Smith (to follow)’.

7160.text: The following rules regarding binding shall apply:

7161.text: (1) All documents, with the exception of transcripts, must be bound together. This may be in a lever arch file, ring binder or plastic folder. Plastic sleeves containing loose documents must not be used. Binders and files must be strong enough to withstand heavy use.

7162.text: (2) Large documents such as plans should be placed in an easily accessible file. Large documents which will need to be opened up frequently should be inserted in a file larger than A4 size.

7165.text: The following rules regarding indices and labels shall apply:

7166.text: (1) An index must be included at the front of the bundle listing all the documents and providing the page references for each. In the case of documents such as letters, invoices or bank statements, they may be given a general description.

7167.text: (2) Where the bundles consist of more than one file, an index to all the files should be included in the first file and an index included for each file. Indices should, if possible, be on a single sheet. The full name of the case should not be inserted on the index if this would waste space. Documents should be identified briefly but properly.

7170.text: The following rules regarding identification shall apply:

7171.text: (1) Every bundle must be clearly identified, on the spine and on the front cover, with the name of the case and the Court $' \mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ reference. Where the bundle consists of more than one file, each file must be numbered on the spine, the front cover and the inside of the front cover.

7172.text: (2) Outer labels should use large lettering e.g. ‘Appeal Bundle A’ or ‘Core Bundle’. The full title of the appeal and legal representatives’G names and addresses should be omitted. A label should be used on the front as well as on the spine.

7175.text: All staples, heavy metal clips, etc., must be removed.

7178.text: The following rules regarding statements of case shall apply:

7179.text: (1) Statements of case should be assembled in ‘chapter’ form — i.e. claim followed by particulars of claim, followed by further information, irrespective of date.

7180.text: (2) Redundant documents, e.g. particulars of claim overtaken by amendments, requests for further information recited in the answers given, should generally be excluded.

7183.text: The following rules regarding new documents shall apply:

7184.text: (1) Before a new document is introduced into bundles which have already been delivered to the CourtG , steps should be taken to ensure that it carries an appropriate bundle/page number so that it can be added to the CourtG documents. It should not be stapled and it should be prepared with punch holes for immediate inclusion in the binders in use.

7185.text: (2) If it is expected that a large number of miscellaneous new documents will from time to time be introduced, there should be a special tabbed empty loose-leaf file for that purpose. An index should be produced for this file, updated as necessary.

7188.text: Since correspondence between legal representativesG is unlikely to be required for the purposes of an appeal, only those letters which will need to be referred to should be copied.

7191.text: If the appellantG fails to comply with the requirements as to the provision of bundles of documents, the application or appeal will be referred for consideration to be given as to why it should not be dismissed for failure so to comply.

7194.text: The appellant $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice must, subject to Rule 44.75, be accompanied by a skeleton argument. Alternatively the skeleton argument may be included in the appellant’sG notice. Where the skeleton argument is so included it will not form part of the notice for the purposes of Rule 44.88.

7196.text: Where it is impracticable for the appellant’sG skeleton argument to accompany the appellant’sG notice it must be filed and served on all respondentsG within 14 days of filingG the notice.

7199.text: A skeleton argument filed on behalf of the appellantG should contain in paragraph 1 the legal representatives’G time estimate for the hearing of the appeal.

7201.text: A skeleton argument must contain a numbered list of the points which the party wishes to make. These should both define and confine the areas of controversy. Each point should be stated as concisely as the nature of the case allows.

7203.text: A numbered point must be followed by a reference to any document on which the party wishes to rely.

7205.text: A skeleton argument must state, in respect of each authority cited:

7206.text: (1) the proposition of law that the authority demonstrates; and

7207.text: (2) the parts of the authority (identified by page or paragraph references) that support the proposition.

7209.text: If more than one authority is cited in support of a given proposition, the skeleton argument must briefly state the reason for taking that course.

7211.text: The statement referred to in Rule 44.80 should not materially add to the length of the skeleton argument

7212.text: but should be sufficient to demonstrate, in the context of the argument:

7213.text: (1) the relevance of the authority or authorities to that argument; and (2) that the citation is necessary for a proper presentation of that argument.

7215.text: The cost of preparing a skeleton argument which:

7216.text: (1) does not comply with the requirements set out in Rules 44.76 to 44.81; or

7217.text: (2) was not filed within the time limits provided by this Part (or any further time granted by the Court);

7218.text: will not be allowed on assessment except to the extent that the CourtG otherwise directs.

7220.text: The appellantG should consider what other information the appeal CourtG will need. This may include a list of persons who feature in the case or glossaries of technical terms. A chronology of relevant events will be necessary in most appeals.

7223.text: Where the judgmentG to be appealed has been officially recorded by the CourtG , an approved transcript of that record should accompany the appellant’sG notice. Photocopies will not be accepted for this purpose. However, where there is no officially recorded judgmentG , the following documents will be acceptable:

7225.text: Where the judgmentG was made in writing a copy of that judgmentG endorsed with the Judge’sG signature.

7227.text: When judgmentG was not officially recorded or made in writing a note of the judgmentG (agreed between the appellant’sG and respondent’sG legal representativesG ) should be submitted for approval to the JudgeG whose decision is being appealed. If the parties cannot agree on a single note of the judgmentG , both versions should be provided to that JudgeG with an explanatory letter. For the purpose of an application for permission to appeal the note need not be approved by the respondentG or the lower CourtG JudgeG .

7228.text: (3) Reasons for judgment in tribunal cases

7229.text: A sealed copy of the tribunal’sG reasons for the decision.

7231.text: An appellantG may not be able to obtain an official transcript or other suitable record of the lower Court’sG

7232.text: decision within the time within which the appellant’sG notice must be filed. In such cases the appellant’sG notice must still be completed to the best of the appellant’sG ability on the basis of the documentation available. However it may be amended subsequently with the permission of the appeal CourtG .

7235.text: When the evidence is relevant to the appeal an official transcript of the relevant evidence must be obtained. Transcripts or notes of evidence are generally not needed for the purpose of determining an application for permission to appeal.

7237.text: If evidence relevant to the appeal was not officially recorded, a typed version of the Judge’sG notes of evidence must be obtained.

7240.text: An appeal noticeG may not be amended without the permission of the appeal CourtG . An application to amend and any application in opposition will normally be dealt with at the appeal hearing unless that course would cause unnecessary expense or delay in which case a request should be made for the application to amend to be heard in advance.

7241.text: Striking Out Appeal Notices And Setting Aside Or Imposing Conditions On Permission To Appeal

7246.text: The CourtG will only exercise its powers under Rule 44.88 where there is a compelling reason for doing so.

7248.text: Where a party was present at the hearing at which permission was given he may not subsequently apply for an order that the CourtG exercise its powers under Rule 44.89(2) or 44.89(3).

7251.text: Rules 44.93 to 44.97 set out the procedure where:

7252.text: (1) permission to appeal is given by the appeal CourtG ; or (2) the appellant’sG notice is filed in the appeal CourtG ; and: (a) permission was given by the lower CourtG ; or (b) permission is not required.

7254.text: If the appeal CourtG gives permission to appeal, the appeal bundle must be served on each of the respondentsG within 7 days of receiving the order giving permission to appeal.

7256.text: The appeal CourtG will send the parties:

7259.text: Where the appeal CourtG grants permission to appeal, the appellantG must add the following documents to the appeal bundle:

7261.text: (4) any document which the appellantG and respondentG have agreed to add to the appeal bundle in accordance with Rule 44.119.

7263.text: Where permission to appeal has been refused on a particular issue, the appellantG must remove from the

7264.text: appeal bundle all documents that are relevant only to that issue.

7267.text: The legal representativesG who will argue the appeal must provide a time estimate within 14 days of the appellantG receiving the order giving permission to appeal. It should exclude the time required by the CourtG to give judgmentG . If the respondentG disagrees with the time estimate, the respondentG must inform the CourtG within 7 days of receipt of the estimate. In the absence of such notification the respondentG will be deemed to have accepted the estimate proposed on behalf of the appellantG .

7270.text: A respondentG may file and serve a respondent’sG notice.

7273.text: (1) is seeking permission to appeal from the appeal CourtG ; or

7274.text: (2) wishes to ask the appeal CourtG to uphold the order of the lower CourtG for reasons different from or additional to those given by the lower CourtG ;

7277.text: Where the respondentG seeks permission from the appeal CourtG it must be requested in the respondent’sG notice.

7279.text: A respondentG who wishes to ask the appeal CourtG to vary the order of the lower CourtG in any way must appeal and permission will be required on the same basis as for an appellantG .

7281.text: A respondentG who wishes only to request that the appeal CourtG upholds the judgmentG or order of the lower CourtG whether for the reasons given in the lower CourtG does not make an appeal and does not therefore require permission to appeal in accordance with Rule 44.5.

7283.text: A respondentG who wishes to appeal or who wishes to ask the appeal CourtG to uphold the order of the

7284.text: lower CourtG for reasons different from or additional to those given by the lower CourtG must file a respondent’sG notice.

7286.text: If the respondentG does not file a respondent’sG notice, he will not be entitled, except with the permission of the CourtG , to rely on any reason not relied on in the lower CourtG .

7289.text: A respondent’sG notice must be filed within:

7290.text: (1) such period as may be directed by the lower CourtG ; or (2) where the CourtG makes no such direction, 14 days after the date in Rule 44.106.

7292.text: The date referred to in Rule 44.105 is:

7293.text: (1) the date the respondentG is served with the appellant’sG notice where:

7294.text: (a) permission to appeal was given by the lower CourtG ; or (b) permission to appeal is not required;

7295.text: (2) the date the respondentG is served with notification that the appeal CourtG has given the appellantG permission to appeal; or

7296.text: (3) the date the respondentG is served with notification that the application for permission to appeal and the appeal itself are to be heard together.

7298.text: Rules 44.41 and 44.42 (extension for time for filingG appellant $' \mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice) also apply to a respondentG and a respondent’sG notice.

7300.text: Where an extension of time is required the extension must be requested in the respondent $\cdot \mathsf { s } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice and the reasons why the respondentG failed to act within the specified time must be included.

7302.text: The respondentG must file a skeleton argument for the CourtG in all cases where he proposes to address arguments to the CourtG . The respondent’sG skeleton argument may be included within a respondent’sG

7303.text: notice. Where a skeleton argument is included within a respondent’sG notice it will not form part of the notice for the purposes of Rule 44.88.

7305.text: Unless the appeal CourtG orders otherwise a respondent’sG notice must be served on the appellantG and any other respondentG :

7306.text: (1) as soon as practicable; and (2) in any event not later than 7 days; after it is filed.

7309.text: (1) files a respondent $\mathsf { \mathsf { S } } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice; but (2) does not include his skeleton argument within that notice; must file and serve his skeleton argument within 14 days of filingG the notice.

7311.text: A respondentG who does not file a respondent’sG notice but who files a skeleton argument must file and serve that skeleton argument at least 7 days before the appeal hearing.

7314.text: (1) serve his skeleton argument on: (a) the appellantG ; and (b) any other respondentG ; at the same time as he files it at the CourtG ; and (2) file a certificate of serviceG .

7317.text: A respondent $\mathsf { S } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ skeleton argument must conform to the directions at Rules 44.76 to 44.83 with any necessary modifications. It should, where appropriate, answer the arguments set out in the appellant’sG

7320.text: A respondentG may include an application within a respondent $\mathsf { \Omega } _ { \mathsf { S } } { } ^ { \mathsf { G } }$ notice in accordance with Rule 44.51 above.

7321.text: Filing Respondent’S Notice And Skeleton Argument

7323.text: The respondentG must file the following documents with his respondent’sG notice in every case:

7324.text: (1) two additional copies of the respondent’sG notice for the appeal CourtG ; and (2) one copy each for the appellantG and any other respondentsG .

7326.text: The respondentG may file a skeleton argument with his respondent’sG notice and—

7327.text: (1) where he does so he must file two copies; and (2) where he does not do so he must comply with Rules 44.111 and 44.112.

7329.text: If the respondentG wishes to rely on any documents which he reasonably considers necessary to enable the appeal CourtG to reach its decision on the appeal in addition to those filed by the appellantG , he must make every effort to agree amendments to the appeal bundle with the appellantG .

7331.text: If the legal representativesG of the parties are unable to reach agreement, the respondentG may prepare a supplemental bundle.

7333.text: If the respondentG prepares a supplemental bundle he must file it, together with the requisite number of copies for the appeal CourtG , at the appeal CourtG :

7335.text: (2) if a respondent’sG notice is not filed, within 21 days after he is served with the appeal bundle.

7339.text: (1) the respondent’sG notice; (2) his skeleton argument (if any); and (3) the supplemental bundle (if any);

7343.text: at the same time as he files them at the CourtG .

7346.text: Once the parties have been notified of the date fixed for the hearing, the appellant’sG legal representativeG must, after consultation with his opponent, file a bundle containing photocopies of the authorities upon which each side will rely at the hearing.

7348.text: The bundle of authorities should, in general:

7351.text: The bundle of authorities must be filed:

7352.text: (1) at least 7 days before the hearing; or (2) where the period of notice of the hearing is less than 7 days, immediately.

7354.text: If, through some oversight, a party intends, during the hearing, to refer to other authorities the parties may agree a second agreed bundle. The appellant’sG legal representativesG must file this bundle at least 48 hours before the hearing commences.

7356.text: A bundle of authorities must bear a certification by the legal representativesG responsible for arguing the case that the requirements of Rule 44.79 to 44.81 have been complied with in respect of each authority included.

7359.text: A supplementary skeleton argument on which the appellantG wishes to rely must be filed at least 14 days before the hearing.

7361.text: A supplementary skeleton argument on which the respondentG wishes to rely must be filed at least 7 days before the hearing.

7363.text: All supplementary skeleton arguments must comply with the requirements set out in Rules 44.76 to 44.83.

7365.text: At the hearing the CourtG may refuse to hear argument from a party not contained in a skeleton argument filed within the relevant time limit set out in this paragraph.

7368.text: All the documents which are needed for the appeal hearing must be filed at least 7 days before the hearing.

7370.text: Any party who fails to comply with the provisions of Rule 44.131 may be required to attend before the Chief Justice G to seek permission to proceed with, or to oppose, the appeal.

7371.text: Appeals To The Court Of Appeal

7373.text: On hearing an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance, the Court of Appeal may:

7375.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance

7377.text: Unless another Rule, Practice Direction or enactment provides otherwise, on hearing an appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

7381.text: Every appeal will be limited to a review of the decision of the lower CourtG unless:

7382.text: (1) the CourtG considers that in the circumstances of an individual appeal it would be in the interests of justice to hold a re-hearing;

7384.text: (3) any other Rule or enactment requires a re-hearing.

7386.text: The hearing of an appeal will be a re-hearing (as opposed to a review of the decision of the lower CourtG ) if the appeal is from the decision of a person or other body and the person or other body:

7387.text:
(1) did not hold a hearing to come to that decision; or   
(2) held a hearing to come to that decision, but the procedure adopted did not provide for the consideration of evidence.

7389.text: The appeal CourtG may exercise its powers in relation to the whole or part of an order of the lower CourtG .

7391.text: At the hearing of the appeal a party may not rely on a matter not contained in his appeal noticeG unless the appeal CourtG gives permission.

7394.text: Unless it orders otherwise, the appeal CourtG will not receive:

7395.text: (1) oral evidence; or (2) evidence which was not before the lower CourtG .

7397.text: Subject to Rule 44.139, the CourtG may receive further evidence, including:

7400.text: The appeal CourtG may draw any inference of fact which it considers justified on the evidence.

7403.text: The Court of Appeal will allow an appeal from a decision of the Court of First Instance where the decision of the lower CourtG was:

7404.text: (1) wrong; or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings in the lower CourtG .

7406.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal from a decision of a tribunalG provided for in the LawG , DIFC Law or Rules of CourtG where the decision was:

7407.text: (1) wrong in relation to a question of law; (2) unjust because of procedural unfairness or a miscarriage of justice; and/or (3) wrong in relation to any other matter provided for in or under DIFC Law.

7409.text: The Court of First Instance will allow an appeal not referred to in Rule 44.143 where the decision was:

7411.text: (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings.

7414.text: The fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into CourtG has been made must not be disclosed to any JudgeG of the appeal CourtG who is to hear or determine:

7415.text: (1) an application for permission to appeal; or

7417.text: until all questions (other than costs) have been determined.

7419.text: Rule 44.145 does not apply if the Part 32 offer or payment into CourtG is relevant to the substance of the appeal.

7421.text: Rule 44.145 does not prevent disclosure in any application in the appeal proceedings if disclosure of the fact that a Part 32 offer or payment into CourtG has been made is properly relevant to the matter to be decided.

7422.text: Who May Exercise The Powers Of The Court Of Appeal

7424.text: The RegistrarG may exercise the jurisdictionG of the Court of Appeal with the consent of the Chief JusticeG to do all things necessary or convenient for the purpose of assisting the JudgesG of the Court of Appeal in the exercise of their powers or duties.

7426.text: A single JudgeG may exercise the appellate jurisdictionG of the Court of Appeal relating to an application for:

7429.text: Decisions of the RegistrarG or a single JudgeG and under Rules 44.148 or 44.149 may be made without a hearing.

7431.text: A party may request any decision of the RegistrarG or the single JudgeG under Rules 44.148 or 44.149 made without a hearing to be reviewed by the Court of Appeal at a hearing.

7433.text: A request under Rule 44.151 must be filed within 7 days after the party is served with notice of the decision.

7435.text: A single JudgeG may refer any matter for a decision by a Court consisting of two or more judgesG .

7436.text: Dismissal Of Applications Or Appeals By Consent

7438.text: Where an appellantG does not wish to pursue an application or an appeal, he may request the appeal CourtG for an order that his application or appeal be dismissed. If such a request is granted it will usually be on the basis that the appellantG pays the costs of the application or appeal.

7440.text: If the appellantG wishes to have the application or appeal dismissed without costs, his request must be accompanied by a consent signed by the respondentG or his legal representativeG stating that the respondentG consents to the dismissal of the application or appeal without costs.

7442.text: Where a settlement has been reached disposing of the application or appeal, the parties may make a joint request to the CourtG asking that the application or appeal be dismissed by consent. If the request is granted the application or appeal will be dismissed.

7443.text: Allowing Unopposed Appeals Or Applications Without A Hearing

7445.text: The appeal CourtG will not normally make an order allowing an appeal unless satisfied that the decision of the lower CourtG was wrong, but the appeal CourtG may set asideG or vary the order of the lower CourtG with consent and without determining the merits of the appeal, if it is satisfied that there are good and sufficient reasons for doing so. Where the appeal CourtG is requested by all parties to allow an application or an appeal the CourtG may consider the request without a hearing. The request should set out the relevant history of the proceedings and the matters relied on as justifying the proposed order and be accompanied by a copy of the proposed order.

7446.text: Availability Of Reserved Judgments Before Hand Down

7448.text: Rules 44.159 and 44.160 apply where the presiding JudgeG is satisfied that the result of the appeal will attract no special degree of confidentiality or sensitivity.

7450.text: A copy of the written judgmentG will be made available to the parties’ legal representativesG by 4 p.m. on the second working day before judgmentG is due to be pronounced or such other period as the CourtG may direct. This can be shown, in confidence, to the parties but only for the purpose of obtaining instructions and on the strict understanding that the judgmentG , or its effect, is not to be disclosed to any other person. A working day is any day on which the Court Office is open for business.

7452.text: The appeal will be listed for judgmentG and the judgmentG handed down at the appropriate time.

7453.text: Attendance Of Legal Representatives On The Handing Down Of A Reserved Judgment

7455.text: Where any consequential orders are agreed, the parties’ legal representatives need not attend on the handing down of a reserved judgmentG . Where a legal representative does attend the CourtG may, if it considers such attendance unnecessary, disallow the costs of the attendance. If the parties do not indicate that they intend to attend, where the appeal was heard by more than one JudgeG , the judgmentG may be handed down by a single member of the CourtG .

7458.text: The parties must, in respect of any draft agreed orders file 4 copies in the Court Office, no later than 12 noon on the working day before the judgmentG is handed down.

7460.text: A copy of a draft order must bear the case reference, the date the judgmentG is to be handed down and the name of the presiding JudgeG .

7463.text: Any proposed correction to the draft judgmentG should be sent to the JudgeG who prepared the draft with a copy to any other party.

7466.text: Costs are likely to be assessed by way of immediate assessment at the following hearings:

7469.text: Parties attending any of the hearings referred to in Rule 44.165 should be prepared to deal with the immediate assessment.

7470.text: Appeals To The Court Of First Instance From The Director Of Employment Standards

7472.text: Rules 44.168 to 44.177 apply to appeals to the Court of First Instance from a determination, decision, or fine of the Director of Employment Standards pursuant to Article 83(1) of the Employment Law, DIFC Law 4 of 2005.

7474.text: An appeal referred to in Rule 44.167 shall be filed within 30 days of receipt of the determination, decision, or fine appealed and in accordance with the requirements of this Part.

7476.text: The Director of Employment Standards must be named as a party to an appeal referred to in Rule 44.167.

7478.text: The CourtG may grant a stayG of the decision appealed from until the disposition of the appeal.

7480.text: The CourtG may attach conditions to the stayG , including requiring a party to deposit as security part or all of a monetary order.

7482.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall provide the CourtG with the record that was before him at the time the determination, order or fine was made, including any witness statement and documents considered by him.

7484.text: The CourtG may consider any other relevant evidence, in addition to the record.

7486.text: Before considering the appeal, the Court of First Instance may:

7487.text: (1) refer the matter back to the Director of Employment Standards for further investigation; or (2) recommend that an attempt be made to settle the matter.

7489.text: The CourtG may dismiss the appeal without a hearing if satisfied that:

7490.text:
(1) the appeal is not within the Court’sG jurisdictionG ;   
(2) the appeal is frivolous or trivial or is not brought in good faith.

7492.text: On hearing the appeal the CourtG may:

7495.text: The Director of Employment Standards shall comply with any directions the CourtG gives to him.

7497.text: 44.178 No appeal lies from a decision of the Court of First Instance or the Court of Appeal on an appeal.

7500.text: The Court of Appeal or the Court of First Instance will not reopen a final determination of any appeal unless:

7501.text:
(1) it is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;   
(2) the circumstances are exceptional and make it appropriate to reopen the appeal; and (3) there is no alternative effective remedy.

7503.text: In Rules 44.179, 44.181, 44.184 and 44.188 “appeal” includes an application for permission to appeal.

7505.text: Permission is needed to make an application under Rule 44.179 to reopen a final determination of an appeal even in cases where permission was not needed for the original appeal.

7507.text: Permission must be sought from the CourtG whose decision the applicant wishes to reopen.

7509.text: The application for permission must be made by application noticeG and supported by written evidence, verified by a statement of truth.

7511.text: A copy of the application for permission must not be served on any other party to the original appeal unless the CourtG so directs.

7513.text: Where the CourtG directs that the application for permission is to be served on another party, that party may within 14 days of the serviceG on him of the copy of the application file and serve a written statement either supporting or opposing the application.

7515.text: The application for permission, and any written statements supporting or opposing it, will be considered on paper by a single JudgeG , and will be allowed to proceed only if the JudgeG so directs.

7517.text: There is no right to an oral hearing of an application for permission unless, exceptionally, the JudgeG so directs.

7519.text: The JudgeG will not grant permission without directing the application to be served on the other party to the original appeal and giving him an opportunity to make representations.

7521.text: There is no right of appeal or review from the decision of the JudgeG on the application for permission, which is final.

7522.text: PART 45 General Rules About Enforcement Of Judgments And Orders

7524.text: Scope Of This Part And Interpretation

7525.text: 45.1 This Part contains general Rules about enforcement of judgments and orders.

7527.text: In this Part and in Parts 46 to 50:

7528.text: (1) ‘judgment creditor’ means a person who has obtained or is entitled to enforce a judgment or order ;

7529.text: (2) ‘judgment debtor’ means a person against whom a judgment or order was given or made;

7530.text: (3) ‘judgment or order’ includes an award which the Court has:

7531.text: (a) ratified for enforcement; (b) ordered to be enforced; or (c) given permission to enforce;

7532.text: as if it were a judgment or order of the Court ; and

7533.text: (4) ‘judgment or order for the payment of money’ includes a judgment or order for the payment of costs, but does not include a judgment or order for the payment of money into Court .

7534.text: Methods Of Enforcing Judgments Or Orders

7536.text: A judgment creditor may enforce a judgment or order for the payment of money by any of the following methods:

7539.text: In addition the Court may make an order of committal for contempt of Court against a judgment debtor but

7540.text: only if permitted by a Rule.

7542.text: The enforcement of a judgment or order may be affected by the Laws and Regulations relating to insolvency.

7544.text: A judgment creditor may, except where an enactment, Rule or Practice Direction provides otherwise:

7545.text: (1) use any method of enforcement which is available; and

7546.text: (2) use more than one method of enforcement, either at the same time or one after another.

7547.text: Enforcement Of Judgment Or Order By Or Against A Non-Party

7549.text: If a judgment or order is given or made in favour of or against a person who is not a party to proceedings, it may be enforced by or against that person by the same methods as if he were a party.

7550.text: Enforcement Of Awards Of Bodies Other Than The Court

7552.text: Rules 45.10 to 45.17 apply, subject to Rule 45.9, if:

7553.text: (1) an award of a sum of money or other decision is made by any court, tribunal , body or person other than the Court ; and

7554.text: (2) an enactment provides that the award may be enforced as if payable under a Court order, or that the decision may be enforced as if it were a Court order.

7556.text: Rules 45.10 to 45.17 do not apply to arbitration awards.

7558.text: If the enactment provides that an award of a sum of money is enforceable if a Court so orders, an application for such an order must be made in accordance with Rules 45.11 to 45.15.

7560.text: An application for an order that an award may be enforced as if payable under a Court order may be made without notice.

7562.text: The application notice must be made by filing an application notice in Form P45/01.
